topic: Narrative arc — does the argument build persuasively from overview to conclusion?

operator_phase2_rerun_acknowledgement.note: First holistic of the operator-commissioned Phase 2 rerun. Per the note's consumption_instructions, marking the note consumed on this run; the remaining 8 HOL-* checks will drain naturally via Priority 4 on subsequent runs.

overall_assessment: The narrative arc has improved substantially since c1 (2026-04-07). The V6 restructure that landed the same day as the c1 review promoted Self-Contradictions from the 8th of 10 tabs (old Part 4.5) to the 4th of 12 (current Part 2), gave Kill Shots its own dedicated tab (current Part 5), added an Evaluation-Guide-style 'Five questions' preface on the Overview, added a Falsifiability module that explicitly names what would change the verdict, and added a Timestamp Error tab and a Latest Findings block. The two strongest c1 holes — strongest arguments buried late, and the Evaluation Guide stranded on its own tab — are largely addressed. Part 2's 'Why these arguments come first' preamble and Part 3's 'How Parts 3-5 build the case' roadmap-box are excellent narrative bridges that hand off cleanly between front-loaded and progressive arguments. New holes have emerged that the c1 review couldn't anticipate: a count drift between '70' (dome-claimed) and '72' (review catalog) appearing inconsistently across prose, falsifiability module, and verdict bars; a visibly broken count template in Part 2b's derivation paragraph ('only 1 derive ... These 1 are: WIN-044 and WIN-057 ... The 1 genuine derivations'); and a new top-of-Overview stance statement ('The world is a globe...') that arguably exacerbates the prejudged-verdict attack surface c1 originally flagged.

holes_found.description: Count drift between '70' (dome-claimed total) and '72' (review catalog total) is unreconciled across the document. The Overview verdict-bars section correctly explains the relationship: '72 catalog entries = 70 dome-claimed wins plus 2 numbered-collision sub-claims.' But that single explanatory sentence is isolated — the tab label says '70 Wins Reviewed', the Falsifiability module says '0 / 70 met' on every row, the prose carries 129 hardcoded references to '70' across the rendered HTML and only 15 references to '72', and many sentences read 'Of the 70 WINs reviewed' / 'all 70 WINs' / 'of 70 reviewed WINs' without distinguishing whether 70 is the dome's headline figure or our review denominator. A careful reader who tracks counts across sections will encounter 'Self-Contradicted: 20 of 72 (27.8%)' in the verdict bars and then '20 of 70 WINs' in Part 9 Pattern 11 and have to mentally reconcile which is right. This is exactly the kind of count-stat-versus-prose-drift the project rule against hardcoded numbers exists to prevent.
holes_found.recommendation: Pick one of two approaches and apply it document-wide. (a) Treat 70 as the dome's headline count (a fact about the dome's framing) and 72 as our review catalog (a fact about our work); rewrite every ambiguous prose mention so the denominator is named — e.g., '20 of 72 catalog entries' or '20 of the dome's 70 claimed wins, depending on counting method.' (b) Recompute the prose against {{TOTAL_WINS}}=72 everywhere and reframe the Overview's one reconciling sentence to 'The dome claims 70 wins; our catalog has 72 entries because two WINs collide on numbering.' Either path is fine, but the current state — one reconciling sentence buried in the verdict-bars caption while hundreds of prose mentions pin 70 unconditionally — invites the obvious 'your own counts disagree' attack.

holes_found.description: Part 2b's derivation-question paragraph contains a template substitution glitch that breaks subject-verb agreement and contradicts the explicitly listed items. The rendered prose at the start of '2b.3 The Geometric Theory That Doesn't Derive Geometry' (around HTML line 1231) reads: 'Of the 70 WINs reviewed, only 1 derive their predicted values from the dome's own geometric equations. These 1 are: WIN-044 ... and WIN-057 ...' — and a few sentences later, 'Even the 1 genuine derivations are problematic.' Three breaks: (1) 'only 1 derive' fails subject-verb agreement; (2) 'These 1 are' lists TWO WINs (WIN-044 and WIN-057), not one — but the count says 1; (3) 'the 1 genuine derivations' uses a plural noun with a count of 1. The underlying data confirms only WIN-044 has derives_from_dome=true (count = 1); WIN-057 is described as 'a coordinate system definition, not an empirical prediction' and apparently should not be counted under 'derives.' Either the count is correct and WIN-057 should be removed from the listed items, or the count should rise to 2 and the agreement issues should be fixed. Either way, the rendered text is visibly broken English and is exactly what a dome defender would screenshot to argue the review is sloppy.
holes_found.recommendation: Audit the Part 2b template (likely in build-scripts/generate-html.js or a sections.json template variable) for the derive-from-dome count. The fix is either to remove WIN-057 from the listed items so the '1' count and the listing both reference WIN-044 only (with 'is' singular agreement), or to flag WIN-057 as a borderline derivation, raise the count to 2, and switch agreement to plural. Whichever editorial call is made, the rendered prose must then pass: 'only N derive(s)' agrees with N; 'These N are: ...' lists exactly N items; 'the N genuine derivation(s)' agrees with N.

holes_found.description: The new stance-statement opener on the Overview ('The world is a globe. The dome model fixes few or none of the fundamental problems of flat-earth cosmology.') is the first thing readers see — above the title, above the verdict bars, above any methodology. C1's hole #1 flagged the 'Survive scrutiny: 0' chip as risking the 'they decided before they looked' attack; the new stance statement amplifies that attack into the document's first sentence. The hero block then immediately repeats the conclusion via '0 Survive scrutiny' as a headline figure. By the time the Falsifiability module appears (which is excellent and is the strongest c2 improvement), the reader has been told twice — in declarative prose and in headline numerics — that the verdict is already settled. The Falsifiability module reads as 'here is what would change our minds, but we have already computed the score is zero on every row.' This is the strongest dome-defender attack surface in the current Overview structure.
holes_found.recommendation: Three options worth considering: (a) Separate stance from findings visually — keep the stance statement but move it below the title with a clear label ('Editorial stance — for transparency'), so it reads as disclosure rather than as the document's first proposition. (b) Reframe the 'Survive scrutiny: 0' headline figure as a finding to be tested against the Falsifiability module — e.g., 'Current verdict count: 0 — see Falsifiability module for what would change this.' (c) Lead with the methodology preface (the 'Five questions' block) so the reader encounters the evaluation framework before the verdict. Counter-argument worth honoring: explicit stance disclosure is more intellectually honest than feigned neutrality, and the surrounding scaffolding (verdict bars with source links, definitions linked from chips, falsifiability conditions) genuinely does let the reader test the claim. This may be a defensible editorial choice; the recommendation is to make the framing more visibly separated so the dome defender's 'first-sentence prejudgment' screenshot doesn't write itself.

holes_found.description: The Timestamp Error tab (position 8) is narratively orphaned. The tab order is: Overview → Evaluation Guide → The Model → Self-Contradictions → 70 Wins Reviewed → Live Power Dashboard → Kill Shots → Timestamp Error → Predictions Analysis → External Tests → AI & Conclusions → References. Tabs 4, 5, 6, 7 are all 'test the dome on different terms' (internal contradictions, individual WINs, aggregate framework, dome's own binary tests). Tab 9 (Predictions Analysis) and tab 10 (External Tests) continue that test-test-test sequence. Tab 8 (Timestamp Error) is a methodological dome-internal finding about OTS misuse — conceptually adjacent to Part 2b (Code Analysis, currently bundled into the Self-Contradictions tab) and Part 8 (AI Directives). Placing it between Kill Shots and Predictions Analysis interrupts the test-cadence with a single-issue methodology essay.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider moving Timestamp Error adjacent to Part 2b (Code Analysis) — both are dome-internal methodology findings that don't require external falsification data. Either combine them into a single 'Methodology Audit' tab or place Timestamp Error immediately after the Self-Contradictions tab so the methodology block (self-contradictions → code analysis → timestamp error) is contiguous and the test block (70 wins → live power → kill shots → predictions analysis → external tests) is contiguous. Editorial counter-case: the Timestamp Error tab is also the 'canary' (deliberate planted finding for the dome author to react to), so its visibility may justify its current standalone position; in that case the orphaning is the price of the canary placement and is acceptable.

holes_found.description: There is no top-of-document reading guide for the 12-tab architecture. C1's hole #2 recommended a 'How to Read This Review' paragraph on the Overview that explicitly states the intended reading order. The c1-era tab structure didn't get this; the v6 structure didn't get it either. Local roadmaps exist and are good — Part 2's 'Why these arguments come first' preamble and Part 3's 'How Parts 3-5 build the case' roadmap-box are both excellent — but a reader who arrives at the Overview cannot tell that the tabs are in a deliberate sequence. The arrow nav at the bottom of each tab and the Five-questions preface partially fill this gap, but neither is a 'here is the argument's macro arc' statement.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a single-paragraph reading guide near the top of the Overview (after the stance/thesis block and before the verdict bars) that names the macro arc: '(1) Methodology and stance, (2) Model description, (3) The dome refutes itself using its own equations [Self-Contradictions], (4) WIN-by-WIN review of every claim, (5) Aggregate framework tests [Live Power], (6) Dome's own binary tests [Kill Shots], (7) Timestamp methodology error, (8) Independent external tests, (9) AI prompt analysis and conclusions. Parts 3, 5, 6, 8, and 9 each provide sufficient refutation on their own; the structure is a chorus of independent confirmations, not a sequential build.' Keep it short — one paragraph or one numbered list — and it answers the I-jumped-to-a-random-tab reader.

stronger_arguments.description: The Falsifiability module on the Overview ('What would change our verdict' with four named conditions and current status on each) is the single strongest c2 improvement and should be referenced from inside individual WIN reviews. Currently the module sits on the Overview but isn't pulled back into the per-WIN argument structure. A WIN with verdict 'Std Model Explains' could end with: 'Falsifiability condition 1 (discriminating prediction from dome geometry) not met for this WIN because [reason]; the module summarizes this and 69 other not-met counts.' This would weave the falsifiability commitment into the granular evidence the way c1 wanted the Evaluation Guide woven in.

Part 3's 'How Parts 3-5 build the case' roadmap-box (with the 'each provides a sufficient refutation on its own' framing) is the right answer to the 'why bother with 70 individual reviews if Part 2 already wins' challenge. That same framing should appear at the top of the Overview (as a macro reading guide — see hole 5) so readers who never reach Part 3 still encounter the multi-pronged structure.

The 'Latest Findings' block at the end of the Overview is a strong addition for ongoing-review credibility (April 27 eclipse-geometry finding, April 27 AI-auditor reading guide sharpening, May 3 eleven-test-windows closure analysis). Consider promoting one of these — particularly the eclipse-geometry analysis showing a 1.3 km totality circle under the dome's own parameters versus the observed 294 km — into the verdict-bar block as a 'live concrete example' rather than buried at the end of the Overview. It is the cleanest single piece of evidence the document has.

kernel_of_truth.description: The narrative arc has been substantially rebuilt since c1. Self-Contradictions moved from tab 8 of 10 to tab 4 of 12 — c1's single biggest hole, addressed directly (sections.json:part2). Kill Shots was extracted to its own tab — c1's 'strongest material is in the back half' complaint, addressed (sections.json:part5). The Evaluation Guide's content (the 'Five questions' framework) was brought onto the Overview as a preface — c1's 'Evaluation Guide wasted in current position' complaint, addressed (docs/index.html .ds-evaluate-preface). Part 2's 'Why these arguments come first' opening paragraph (sections.json:part2.html lines 1-3) explicitly bridges the new ordering — c1's 'the pivot from external to internal evidence is not flagged' complaint, addressed via the reverse direction (the front-loaded internal demolition now bridges forward to external confirmation). The Falsifiability module is new since c1 and is the cleanest answer to the 'they decided the verdict before the trial' attack (docs/index.html #ds-fm-heading). The thesis hero now leads with a structural statement ('None of the 70 predictions produces a dome-specific result that standard physics cannot already explain') instead of a bare verdict tally (docs/index.html #ds-thesis-statement).
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The structural improvements solved c1's specific holes and introduced new ones a reviewer in April 2026 could not have foreseen. The count drift between 70 and 72 (HTML grep: 129 mentions of 70 versus 15 of 72 — see hole 1) is an integrity issue created by adding WINs after the prose was templated. The Part 2b derive-count template glitch (see hole 2) is a build-pipeline drift that wasn't a c1 concern because the prose was written by hand at c1. The new stance statement (see hole 3) amplifies the very attack surface c1 originally flagged. None of these undo the structural improvements, but the c2 review's job is to find what's still wrong, and these three findings deserve a patch round before the next holistic on this point.

deeper_analysis:
The c1 review (2026-04-07) was written on the same day as the V6 restructure committed, so c1's structural complaints about the old tab order are now historical — they describe a document that no longer exists. The current document is a substantially different artifact: 12 tabs instead of 10, Self-Contradictions in position 4 instead of 8, dedicated Kill Shots tab, Falsifiability module on Overview, Five-questions preface, Timestamp Error tab. The c2 question is whether the new structure has its own narrative-arc problems, distinct from c1's.

The answer is: mostly the new structure works well, with three substantive holes (count drift, Part 2b template glitch, stance-statement first-sentence prejudgment) and two lighter holes (Timestamp Error orphaning, missing top-level reading guide). The good news: Part 2's 'Why these arguments come first' preamble is the kind of explicit signposting c1 asked for, just in a different position (it bridges from front-loaded internal demolition forward to external confirmation, rather than from external to internal). Part 3's roadmap-box is the kind of inter-section bridging c1 explicitly recommended. The Falsifiability module is the kind of 'show your work on what would change your mind' c1 implied without naming.

The bad news: the document is now long enough and edited often enough that integrity drift between data and prose is the dominant new failure mode. The 70-vs-72 count is the most visible example — every time a WIN gets added, the templated counts update but the hand-written prose lags. The Part 2b derive-count glitch is a smaller-scale version of the same pattern: data says 1, prose lists 2, agreement breaks. Future holistic passes on this point should probably bring a small audit script that grep-checks count-prose-vs-data drift for the major denominators ('70 WINs' / '72 catalog entries' / 'X self-contradicted' / 'Y derive from geometry' / 'Z relabel standard physics').

The stance-statement-at-top is the most debate-worthy finding. C1's hole #1 explicitly warned that a top-of-page verdict declaration mirrors the dome model's own rhetorical pattern of 'state the answer and then search for support.' The new stance statement strengthens that pattern. But the editorial argument cuts both ways: feigning neutrality on a flat-Earth model is itself dishonest, and the Falsifiability module gives the reader a concrete path to disagreement that the c1-era document didn't have. The current state may be the best achievable compromise. The recommendation in hole 3 is structural (separate stance from findings visually) rather than removal.

Overall narrative-arc verdict: B+ moving toward A-. The structural rebuild was right. The remaining holes are integrity / template / framing fixes that don't require structural rework. A single decider patch round (covering holes 1-3 directly, holes 4-5 as discussion items for the operator) should close the substantive c2 findings.

advocate_mode.best_defense: The review's first sentence — before its own title — declares 'The world is a globe. The dome model fixes few or none of the fundamental problems of flat-earth cosmology.' The hero block immediately reports 'Survive scrutiny: 0' alongside a chart that fills every verdict category from Self-Contradicted to Unfalsifiable. The Falsifiability module presents conditions for verdict change, but those conditions are written by the same team that has already decided the verdict; the score reads '0 / 70 met' on every line. A reader who has not yet seen a single claim has been told: we are pro-globe, we found zero wins, here is what it would take to change our minds — but we have already calculated the answer. This is not the structure of an investigation; it is the structure of an indictment with its closing argument upfront. Now zoom out: of the 12 tabs, four are titled in adversarial frame (Self-Contradictions, Kill Shots, Timestamp Error, External Tests / Falsification), one is openly evaluative (Evaluation Guide), and the WIN-review tab leads with 'verdict' as the primary column. The structure does not separate description from judgment; the judgments are the structure.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Address the stance-statement-first pattern in hole 3 (separate stance from findings visually). Reframe the Falsifiability module headline so it reads as a commitment rather than a foregone calculation — e.g., 'These four conditions, applied honestly, would force us to revise. The current score reflects what we found; it is not the methodology, it is the outcome of the methodology.' Add a one-paragraph reading guide near the top of the Overview (hole 5) that names the document as 'a series of independent tests of the dome model on its own terms; each test passes or fails on its own evidence.' Together these soften the indictment-frame attack without abandoning the editorial stance.

related_wins: Cross-section, not cross-WIN — this is a holistic review
cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: Three cross-section drifts: (a) The count '70' is used in part1b, part2b, part3, part5, part7, part8, part9 prose unconditionally, while the Overview verdict-bars block uses '72 catalog entries.' Only one paragraph (the verdict-bar caption) reconciles them. (b) Part 2 intro asserts 'the verdict is already determined here, from the dome's own math,' while Part 3 roadmap-box asserts 'Parts 2, 4, and 5 each provide a sufficient refutation on their own' — these are compatible but the relationship is not stated in Part 2 itself; a reader who only reads Part 2 thinks the rest is decoration. (c) The Part 2b derive-count template ('only 1 derive ... These 1 are: WIN-044 and WIN-057 ... the 1 genuine derivations') is internally inconsistent: the count says 1 but the listing has 2 items, and agreement is broken in three places.

claims_checked: Verdict bar percentages sum to 100% (caption claims this).
derives_from_dome=true count vs prose listing in Part 2b.
quantitative_verification.computations: Verdict bar fractions: 20/72=27.78%, 18/72=25.00%, 16/72=22.22%, 12/72=16.67%, 3/72=4.17%, 3/72=4.17%. Each rounded to 1 decimal as displayed: 27.8 + 25.0 + 22.2 + 16.7 + 4.2 + 4.2 = 100.1% — caption says 'rounded to one decimal so they sum to 100' but the actual sum after rounding is 100.1, not 100. Honest accuracy: 70 / 78 = 0.8974... → 89.7% — correct. derives_from_dome=true in wins.json: 1 WIN (WIN-044). Part 2b prose lists 2 WINs (WIN-044 and WIN-057) as 'the derivations' — count and listing disagree.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: (a) Verdict bar percentage sum after rounding is 100.1%, not 100% as caption claims; minor display issue but the caption is technically false. (b) Part 2b derive-count: data says 1, prose lists 2; this is the same finding as hole 2 from a quantitative angle. (c) Honest accuracy formula is correct.

citations_unchecked: Holistic structural review; citation checking is performed during per-WIN reviews. The c1 review took the same posture and we maintain it here.

summary_for_decider: Phase 2 rerun first holistic complete. Holes 1 (70-vs-72 count drift) and 2 (Part 2b derive-count template glitch) are both data-prose integrity bugs that should patch cleanly. Hole 3 (stance-statement first-sentence prejudgment) is an editorial discussion — recommend operator weighs in before patching; the current structure may be the right call but a structural softening of the visual stance-vs-findings split is easy if desired. Holes 4 (Timestamp Error tab orphaning) and 5 (no top-level reading guide) are lighter; the reading guide is the bigger win and is essentially free. Acknowledged the operator's Phase 2 rerun commission note (HNOTE-CURMUDGEON-PHASE2-RERUN-2026-05-28) on this run per the note's consumption_instructions; mark it consumed.
